## Context and Problem Statement

A solution for isolating third-party payment widgets from the rest of account
management can include building a separate web app on its own dedicated domain.
As a side effect in building that app, we have the opportunity to choose
technologies for building it that don't necessarily follow the rest of FxA.

## Decision Drivers

* Opportunity for a fresh start with tech stack without rewriting
* Security in dealing with payment transactions
* Developer ergonomics
* Code quality & testing
* Subscription services deadlines

## Considered Options

* React, Redux, Typescript
* Backbone.js
human_decision:
# Use React, Redux, and Typescript for subscription management pages

* Deciders: Ben Bangert, Ian Bicking, Les Orchard
* Date: 2019-05-08

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "React, Redux, Typescript", because

* Chance to start with a fresh stack
* More vibrant ecosystem
* Better tooling & developer ergonomics

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### React, Redux, TypeScript

[React][] is a library for building DOM-based UI components in a data-driven,
declarative style that minimizes granular DOM manipulation by developers by way
of a Virtual DOM diff / patch algorithm. It also introduces patterns & tools for
encapsulating reusable UI functionality in "hooks" and JS classes.

[React]: https://reactjs.org/

[Redux][] is a library for managing app state in a singleton container using a
defined inventory of actions that describe changes to be applied via reducer
functions and middleware filters.

[Redux]: https://redux.js.org/

[TypeScript][] is a superset of JavaScript which introduces type annotation
expressions and type inference along with tooling to lint and compile.

[TypeScript]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/

We can also consider other dependencies that act as glue between these pieces
and utilities to reduce boilerplate and repetition. (e.g. [redux-actions][] to
simplify constructing actions; [redux-promise-middleware][] for actions that
result in async API fetches; [redux-thunk][] for producing action sequences)

[redux-actions]: https://redux-actions.js.org/ 

[redux-promise-middleware]:
https://github.com/pburtchaell/redux-promise-middleware 

[redux-thunk]: https://github.com/reduxjs/redux-thunk

* Pros
  * Frequently updated and vibrant ecosystem
  * New team members may be more familiar with the React / Redux / TypeScript
    ecosystem
  * React integrates well with Webpack for code-splitting and lazy-loaded
    component JS bundles
  * React and Webpack can support per-component hot reloading rather than
    reloading the whole page
  * React can organize UI rendering into small components with single
    responsibility for each
  * React hooks can be used to encapsulate reusable chunks of UI logic & state
    between components
  * React offers [safeguards][react-innerhtml] against accidental XSS from
    unescaped HTML.
  * Redux can organize business logic and API requests in a central set of
    modules
  * Data-driven React components can be loosely-coupled with Redux state
  * The Redux dev tools browser add-on can monitor, inspect, and even rewind app
    state changes
  * TypeScript can help document & validate data structures at build time (e.g.
    in Redux state) and integrate with IDEs (e.g. intellisense hints in VS Code)
  * Can follow the Mocha / Chai / Sinon style unit testing stack used elsewhere
    in the project
  * Can use [React bindings][fluent-react] for [fluent.js][fluent] and [FTL][]
    strings for L10n.
* Cons
  * Novel technology stack for the project in general.
  * Fast-moving tech stack that may need chasing after.
  * Reduced code sharing with the rest of the project, reinventing some wheels.
  * Fluent is a research project by Mozilla, [it has occasionally presented
    challenges][fluent-challenges].
  
[fluent]: https://projectfluent.org/
[fluent-react]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent.js/wiki/React-Bindings
[ftl]: https://projectfluent.org/fluent/guide/
[fluent-challenges]: https://github.com/mozilla/testpilot/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed+fluent
[react-innerhtml]: https://reactjs.org/docs/dom-elements.html#dangerouslysetinnerhtml

### Backbone.js

[Backbone.js][] is small framework that offers base View and Model abstractions.

[Backbone.js]: https://backbonejs.org/

FxA combines it with a few other utilities including [Mustache][] for HTML
templates; [Cocktail][] for class mixins; [jQuery][] for DOM manipulation.
Depends on [Underscore.js][] for general data manipulation.

[Mustache]: https://mustache.github.io/ 

[Cocktail]: https://github.com/onsi/cocktail

[jQuery]: https://jquery.com/

[Underscore.js]: https://underscorejs.org/

* Pros
  * Framework of record for client-side code in FxA so far.
  * Granular DOM updates in View classes do not rely on an abstract Virtual DOM
    that can have an impact on performance.
  * Pretty small framework, even with the added utilities.
  * Mature framework that doesn't need much chasing after.
  * Lots of mature tested code within FxA to reuse and on which to model new
    additions.
  * Uses [mature gettext tools & processes for L10n][fxa-l10n].
  * Mustache and Backbone best practices help guard against XSS via unescaped HTML.
* Cons
  * New team members may not be familiar with Backbone patterns
  * Updating Views requires granular DOM changes that can sometime collide
    between different Views that affect the same parts of the DOM - i.e
    overlapping responsibilities.
  * Business logic can be scattered amongst separate Model classes.
  * Infrequent releases: v1.4.0 released on 2019-02, but v1.3.3 released on
    2016-04.
  * fxa-content-server uses v1.1.1 from 2014-02 along with similarly-aged
    versions of Cocktail and Mustache.
  * Fluent [was created][fluent-created] to address issues [found over years of using
    gettext][fluent-v-gettext] at Mozilla.

[fxa-l10n]: https://github.com/mozilla/fxa-content-server-l10n/
[fluent-created]: https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/04/fluent-1-0-a-localization-system-for-natural-sounding-translations/
[fluent-v-gettext]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent/wiki/Fluent-vs-gettext
debate_answer: React, Redux, and Typescript are the better choice for building a separate web app for isolating third-party payment widgets, considering the decision drivers and long-term benefits.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for React, Redux, and Typescript, emphasizing their modernity, scalability, and strong community support. These factors align well with the decision drivers, particularly in terms of security, code quality, and long-term maintainability. While the negative side highlights the simplicity and speed of Backbone.js, the potential drawbacks of using an outdated framework with limited scalability and community support outweigh the benefits, especially for a project involving payment transactions.
message_history:
Let's evaluate the pros and cons of each considered solution:

### React, Redux, Typescript

**Pros:**

1. **Modern Tech Stack:** React, Redux, and Typescript are widely used and considered modern technologies. They offer a fresh start and are well-suited for building scalable and maintainable applications.

2. **Component-Based Architecture:** React's component-based architecture promotes reusability and modularity, which can enhance code quality and maintainability.

3. **Strong Community Support:** React and Redux have large communities, which means abundant resources, libraries, and tools are available. This can improve developer ergonomics and speed up development.

4. **Type Safety:** Typescript adds type safety to JavaScript, reducing runtime errors and improving code quality.

5. **Testing Tools:** There are robust testing tools and frameworks available for React and Typescript, which can enhance code quality and testing.

6. **Security:** React's virtual DOM and Typescript's type safety can contribute to a more secure application, especially important for handling payment transactions.

**Cons:**

1. **Learning Curve:** Developers unfamiliar with React, Redux, or Typescript may face a steep learning curve, which could impact deadlines.

2. **Complexity:** The combination of React, Redux, and Typescript can introduce complexity, especially for small teams or projects.

3. **Overhead:** The setup and configuration of a React/Redux/Typescript project can be more involved compared to simpler frameworks.

### Backbone.js

**Pros:**

1. **Simplicity:** Backbone.js is a lightweight framework that is easy to understand and use, which can be beneficial for meeting tight deadlines.

2. **Minimalistic:** It provides the minimal structure needed for web applications, which can reduce overhead and complexity.

3. **Familiarity:** If the team has prior experience with Backbone.js, it could lead to faster development and easier maintenance.

**Cons:**

1. **Outdated:** Backbone.js is considered outdated compared to modern frameworks like React. It lacks some of the advanced features and community support that newer technologies offer.

2. **Limited Community and Resources:** The community around Backbone.js is smaller, which means fewer resources, libraries, and tools are available.

3. **Scalability Issues:** Backbone.js may not be as scalable or maintainable as React, especially for larger applications.

4. **Testing and Code Quality:** Backbone.js does not inherently provide the same level of support for testing and code quality as React and Typescript.

### Conclusion

Considering the decision drivers, React, Redux, and Typescript seem to be the better choice. They offer a modern tech stack with strong community support, which can improve developer ergonomics, code quality, and security. While there is a learning curve and some complexity, the long-term benefits in terms of maintainability, scalability, and security outweigh the initial challenges. Additionally, the robust testing tools available for this stack can help ensure high code quality, which is crucial for handling payment transactions securely.
While I understand the inclination towards React, Redux, and Typescript due to their modernity and robust features, I would like to present a counterargument in favor of Backbone.js, considering the specific context and decision drivers.

### Backbone.js

**Pros:**

1. **Simplicity and Speed:** Backbone.js is lightweight and straightforward, which can be advantageous for quickly setting up a project. This is particularly beneficial given the subscription services deadlines, as it allows for rapid development and deployment.

2. **Lower Learning Curve:** For teams already familiar with Backbone.js, the learning curve is minimal, which can lead to faster onboarding and development. This can be crucial when deadlines are tight.

3. **Minimal Overhead:** Backbone.js provides just enough structure without the overhead of more complex frameworks. This can lead to faster performance and easier debugging, which is important for maintaining developer ergonomics.

**Cons:**

1. **Outdated Technology:** While Backbone.js is considered outdated, it still serves well for smaller, less complex applications. If the project scope is limited, Backbone.js can be a viable option.

2. **Limited Features:** The lack of advanced features might be a drawback, but it also means fewer moving parts, which can simplify maintenance and reduce potential security vulnerabilities.

3. **Community Support:** Although the community is smaller, Backbone.js is a mature framework with stable documentation, which can still support development needs.

### React, Redux, Typescript

**Cons:**

1. **Complexity and Overhead:** The complexity and setup overhead of React, Redux, and Typescript can be significant, especially for a project that aims to isolate third-party payment widgets. This could lead to delays and increased development time, which might conflict with subscription services deadlines.

2. **Resource Intensive:** The modern stack requires more resources in terms of both development and runtime, which could be a consideration if the project needs to be lightweight and efficient.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers, particularly the subscription services deadlines and the opportunity for a fresh start without rewriting, Backbone.js could be a more pragmatic choice. Its simplicity and minimalistic nature allow for rapid development and deployment, which aligns well with tight deadlines. While it may not offer the same level of modern features as React, Redux, and Typescript, it provides a stable and efficient framework for smaller, isolated applications like third-party payment widgets.

Ultimately, the choice depends on the specific project requirements, team expertise, and long-term goals. If the project scope is limited and deadlines are a priority, Backbone.js could be the better fit. However, if scalability and future-proofing are more critical, React, Redux, and Typescript would be the way to go.

## Context and Problem Statement

In the context of classic services' requirement for contact HMRC and Welsh information links in their footer, facing the
 fact that these links are missing from hmrcStandardFooter, should we add them?

The additional links needed are:

* "Contact", linking to https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/hm-revenue-customs/contact
* "Rhestr o Wasanaethau Cymraeg", linking to https://www.gov.uk/cymraeg

## Decision Drivers

* The need for consistency across HMRC services.
* Our belief that including them is likely to improve the user experience for tax users.
* We can see no good reason for not including them as standard because they are applicable across HMRC services.
* We have a time sensitive opportunity of acting now while teams are in the process of
uplifting their frontend libraries.
* The HMRC design community have been consulted on multiple public Slack channels and two
successive design system working group meetings, with no objections noted.
* Classic services support multiple live services. Not including these links as standard would mean their having to
duplicate these links, and associated English and Welsh content, across tens of repositories.

## Considered Options

* Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter
* Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links
* Do nothing
human_decision:
# Add contact HMRC and Welsh information links to standard footer

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-12-04

Technical Story: PLATUI-854

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter", because this 
will benefit tax users, and we have a unique window of opportunity to act now.

### Positive Consequences

* Tax users have better information provided to them
* Teams do not need to duplicate content and URLs across hundreds of repositories
* We can more easily maintain the content and links in a central repository

### Negative Consequences

* Teams currently using a Welsh link as their language toggle will likely need to switch to using one of the standard components
for language switching e.g. hmrcLanguageSelect.
* Teams already including a contact link manually will need to remove it when upgrading. 

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter

* Good, because it's more likely the additional links will become standard benefiting
users
* Good, because we can standardise the content for English and Welsh
* Good, because hyperlinks can be maintained in a central place and do not have to be corrected in 100s of separate services
* Bad, because it's possible some teams may not want a contact or Welsh link for valid business reasons

### Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links

* Good, because new links will be added consistently for teams adopting it
* Good, because we can standardise the text for English and Welsh
* Good, because the hyperlinks can be maintained in a central repository
* Bad, because uptake unlikely to be as high as making the links standard
* Bad, because teams may become confused about which footer to use, increasing delivery friction

### Do nothing

* Good, because teams have more flexibility
* Bad, because tax users will not benefit from these additional information links
* Bad, because teams may add the additional links inconsistently with different text, different URLs, or 
 in a different order
* Bad, because there is no standardisation of the content in English and Welsh
* Bad, because if changes need to be made, they will need to be made in 100s of individual services rather
than in one library
* Bad, because teams will have to rollback these changes if they adopt a new standard footer with the contact and Welsh links
debate_answer: The best solution is to add the links to hmrcStandardFooter, as it ensures consistency, improves user experience, and reduces duplication across repositories, aligning with the decision drivers and community support.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for adding the links to the hmrcStandardFooter, emphasizing the importance of consistency, improved user experience, and efficiency. These factors align closely with the decision drivers, and the approach is supported by the HMRC design community. While the negative side offers valid points about flexibility and future-proofing, the potential for inconsistency and complexity outweighs these benefits. The affirmative solution provides a straightforward and widely supported approach that meets the current needs effectively.

Let's evaluate each of the considered options by listing their pros and cons:

### Option 1: Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter

**Pros:**
1. **Consistency:** Ensures that all HMRC services have a consistent footer, which aligns with the decision driver for consistency.
2. **User Experience:** Likely to improve the user experience by providing easy access to contact information and Welsh services, which is a key decision driver.
3. **Efficiency:** Reduces the need for duplication across multiple repositories, saving time and effort for developers.
4. **Simplicity:** A straightforward solution that integrates seamlessly into the existing system without the need for additional components.
5. **Community Support:** No objections from the HMRC design community, indicating general agreement with this approach.

**Cons:**
1. **Increased Footer Size:** Adding more links could make the footer more cluttered, potentially impacting the visual design.
2. **Maintenance:** Any future changes to these links would require updates to the standard footer across all services.

### Option 2: Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links

**Pros:**
1. **Flexibility:** Allows services to choose whether to include the additional links, providing more control over the footer content.
2. **Customization:** Services that do not require these links can opt to use the standard footer, keeping their design minimal.

**Cons:**
1. **Inconsistency:** Could lead to inconsistency across HMRC services if some use the extended footer and others do not.
2. **Complexity:** Introduces an additional component, which could complicate the design system and increase the maintenance burden.
3. **Duplication:** Services that choose not to use the extended footer would still need to duplicate the links, counteracting the efficiency goal.

### Option 3: Do nothing

**Pros:**
1. **No Immediate Changes:** Maintains the current system without requiring any updates or changes.

**Cons:**
1. **Inconsistency:** Fails to address the need for consistency across HMRC services.
2. **User Experience:** Misses the opportunity to improve the user experience by not providing easy access to important links.
3. **Duplication:** Requires services to duplicate the links across multiple repositories, leading to inefficiencies.
4. **Missed Opportunity:** Does not take advantage of the current opportunity to update the frontend libraries.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers and the pros and cons of each option, the best solution is to **add the links to hmrcStandardFooter**. This option aligns with the need for consistency, improves user experience, and reduces duplication across repositories. It is also supported by the HMRC design community and takes advantage of the current opportunity to update the frontend libraries. While it may slightly increase the footer size, the benefits of consistency and improved user experience outweigh this drawback.
While I understand the reasoning behind selecting the option to add the links to the hmrcStandardFooter, I would like to present an alternative perspective that supports the creation of an hmrcExtendedFooter component.

### Option 2: Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links

**Pros:**
1. **Flexibility:** This option provides flexibility for different services to choose whether they want to include the additional links. This can be particularly beneficial for services that may not require these links due to their specific user base or context.
2. **Customization:** By allowing services to opt for the standard or extended footer, this approach supports a more tailored user experience. Services that do not need the additional links can maintain a cleaner, more focused footer design.
3. **Future-Proofing:** Introducing an extended footer component allows for easier future updates or additions. If more links or information need to be added later, they can be incorporated into the extended footer without affecting the standard footer.

**Cons:**
1. **Inconsistency:** There is a risk of inconsistency if some services use the extended footer and others do not. However, this can be managed through clear guidelines and recommendations for when to use each footer type.
2. **Complexity:** While it introduces an additional component, the complexity can be mitigated with proper documentation and support for developers.

### Why Option 2 Might Be the Best Solution

1. **Balancing Consistency and Flexibility:** While consistency is important, flexibility is also crucial in accommodating the diverse needs of different services. The extended footer allows for a balance between these two factors.
2. **User-Centric Approach:** By allowing services to choose the most appropriate footer for their users, this option supports a more user-centric approach, potentially enhancing the user experience for specific audiences.
3. **Scalability:** As HMRC services evolve, the extended footer provides a scalable solution that can adapt to future needs without requiring significant changes to the standard footer.

In conclusion, while adding the links to the hmrcStandardFooter is a valid option, creating an hmrcExtendedFooter component offers a more flexible and future-proof solution. It allows for customization and scalability while still supporting consistency through clear guidelines. This approach can better accommodate the diverse needs of HMRC services and their users.

## Context and Problem Statement

It is the offical policy of the Digital Iceland and stated in the Techical Direction that it is to be implemented as free and open source. Open source software by definition is open to anyone to use, modify, distribute and study. These permissions are enforced an open source license. There are a number of well-known and widely used open source licenses available and we need to choose a license that best fits the goals of digital iceland.

There are two main types of open source licences: more permissive licences that confer broad freedoms and minimal obligations (e.g., the MIT, BSD and the Apache 2.0 licences); and sharealike licences that require licensing adaptations with the same licence if they distribute them (e.g., the GNU GPL).

Development for Digital Iceland will be open and free with minimum complications for development for all involved. Reuse and transparency will be promoted.

## Decision Drivers

- The primary motivation is to encourage, co-development, collabiration, transparency and reuse of the software.
- It is important to build on the experience of similar government led inititives in other countries.
- Digital Iceland has no patents or intellecatual property that needs to be protected or guarded by the license chosen.
- It is not a concern for Digital Iceland that the license restricts usage in other projects, be it open or closed source.

## Considered Options

The different licenses

- Apache
- BSD
- GNU GPL
- MIT
human_decision:
# Open Source License

- Status: proposed
- Deciders: dev, devops, managers
- Date: 2020-06-07

## Decision Outcome

The MIT license was chosen, for the following reasons:

- It is the least restrictive of the licenses.
- It is very consise, simple and easy to understand and therefore should be clear to users and developers.
- Digital Iceland does not require protection of patents or existing intelletual property.
- Well known government lead initiatives like uk.gov and X-Road use the MIT license.
- The MIT license is the best known and most widely used free and open-source license in the world.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Apache

- Good, because is well known and very permissive like the MIT license.
- Bad, it is has restrictions around redistribution that do not apply for Digital Iceland.
- Bad, is way very long and wordy and therefore requires more effort to understand.

### BSD

- Good, because is well known and very permissive like the MIT license.
- Bad, because it has restrictions about using the names of the copyright holder which is not a concern for digital Iceland.

### GNU GPL

- Good, because it is very well known.
- Bad, that it is not permissive and requires derived software to adopt the license as well.

## Links

- [Stjórnarráðið - Umfjöllun um opinn hugbúnað](https://www.stjornarradid.is/verkefni/upplysingasamfelagid/stafraent-frelsi/opinn-hugbunadur)
- [Ríkisendurskoðun - Frjáls og opinn hugbúnaðr](https://rikisendurskodun.is/wp-content/uploads/2016/01/Frjals_og_opinn_hugbunadur_01.pdf)
debate_answer: The Apache License is the best choice for Digital Iceland due to its patent protection, structured attribution, and alignment with government initiatives, which support the project's goals of co-development, collaboration, transparency, and reuse.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for the Apache License, emphasizing its patent protection, which could be beneficial for future-proofing the project. Additionally, the requirement for attribution and documentation aligns well with the goal of promoting transparency. The Apache License's balance of permissiveness and structure, along with its alignment with government initiatives, makes it a strong candidate for Digital Iceland. These factors outweigh the simplicity of the MIT License, especially considering the potential long-term benefits of the Apache License.
message_history:
To determine the best open source license for Digital Iceland, let's evaluate the pros and cons of each considered option:

### Apache License

**Pros:**
- **Permissive:** Allows for broad use, modification, and distribution, including in proprietary software.
- **Patent Protection:** Provides an express grant of patent rights from contributors to users.
- **Attribution:** Requires that modifications are documented, which can enhance transparency.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** Slightly more complex than other permissive licenses like MIT or BSD.
- **Less Viral:** Does not require derivative works to be open source, which might not fully align with the goal of promoting transparency.

### BSD License

**Pros:**
- **Permissive:** Very simple and permissive, allowing for broad use, modification, and distribution.
- **Simplicity:** Easy to understand and implement, with minimal restrictions.

**Cons:**
- **No Patent Protection:** Does not include an express grant of patent rights, which might be a concern for some developers.
- **Less Viral:** Like Apache, it does not require derivative works to be open source.

### GNU GPL

**Pros:**
- **Copyleft:** Requires that derivative works are also open source, promoting transparency and reuse.
- **Community Focused:** Encourages collaboration and sharing improvements with the community.

**Cons:**
- **Restrictive:** More restrictive than permissive licenses, which might complicate collaboration with proprietary software.
- **Complexity:** More complex to understand and implement compared to permissive licenses.

### MIT License

**Pros:**
- **Permissive:** Extremely simple and permissive, allowing for broad use, modification, and distribution.
- **Simplicity:** Very easy to understand and implement, with minimal restrictions.

**Cons:**
- **No Patent Protection:** Like BSD, it does not include an express grant of patent rights.
- **Less Viral:** Does not require derivative works to be open source.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers for Digital Iceland, the best choice seems to be the **MIT License**. It aligns well with the goals of encouraging co-development, collaboration, transparency, and reuse of the software. Its simplicity and permissiveness make it easy for developers to adopt and contribute to the project without legal complexities. Additionally, since Digital Iceland has no patents or intellectual property concerns, the lack of patent protection in the MIT License is not a significant drawback. The MIT License's minimal restrictions also ensure that the software can be used in a wide range of projects, both open and closed source, which aligns with the stated lack of concern about usage restrictions.
While the MIT License is indeed a strong candidate due to its simplicity and permissiveness, I would argue that the **Apache License** might be a better fit for Digital Iceland, considering the decision drivers and goals outlined.

### Reasons for Choosing the Apache License

1. **Patent Protection:**
   - The Apache License includes an express grant of patent rights from contributors to users. While Digital Iceland currently has no patents or intellectual property concerns, this feature could be beneficial in the future if the project expands or if contributors have patents related to their contributions. It provides an additional layer of security and assurance for users and developers.

2. **Attribution and Documentation:**
   - The Apache License requires that modifications are documented and that attribution is given to the original authors. This aligns with the goal of promoting transparency, as it ensures that changes are tracked and that credit is given where due. This can enhance the collaborative environment by recognizing contributors' efforts.

3. **Permissiveness with Structure:**
   - While the Apache License is slightly more complex than the MIT License, it still offers broad freedoms for use, modification, and distribution, including in proprietary software. This balance of permissiveness and structure can encourage co-development and collaboration without imposing significant restrictions.

4. **Alignment with Government Initiatives:**
   - The Apache License is widely used in government and large-scale projects, providing a precedent that Digital Iceland can build upon. This experience from similar initiatives can be valuable in ensuring the project's success and acceptance.

### Conclusion

The Apache License offers a good balance between permissiveness and protection, making it a strong candidate for Digital Iceland. It supports the project's goals of encouraging co-development, collaboration, transparency, and reuse while providing additional benefits like patent protection and structured attribution. These features can enhance the project's credibility and sustainability in the long term, making the Apache License a compelling choice.

## Context and Problem Statement

As of the date of this writing, there are multiple different naming styles used in the monorepo, mostly because NX has defaults that differ between schematic types. In order for navigating the monorepo in a consistent rational manner, we should align on naming strategy for files and directories.

## Decision Drivers

- Provide consistency when navigating the codebase
- The earlier we decide on this, the better

## Considered Options

Some mixture of these:

- kebab-case
- PascalCase
- camelCase
- snake_case
human_decision:
# Unified Naming Strategy for Files and Directories

- Status: accepted
- Deciders: devs
- Date: 2020-07-03

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: Name files after their default export. If that default export is a React Component, or a class, then the file name should be in PascalCase. Otherwise, the filename should be in camelCase. Basically, for naming files avoid using kebab-case and snake_case and make sure the name follows the default export of the file.

Naming directories should follow these guidelines: Only use kebab-case when naming NX apps and libraries, or folders containing apps and libraries, e.g. `island-ui` instead of `islandUi`: `import { Box } from '@island.is/island-ui/core'`

Use PascalCase for directories only containing React components:

```text
components/CtaButton/index.ts
import 'components/CtaButton'
```

or:

```text
components/CtaButton/CtaButton.tsx
import 'components/CtaButton/CtaButton'
```

rather than

```text
components/cta-button/CtaButton.tsx
```

In all other cases, use camelCase.

### Positive Consequences

- Easier to navigate the codebase
- File names are more readable, and developers know what to expect
- This approach is the most common practice, and something most JS and TS developers are familiar with.
debate_answer: The best option for file and directory naming in the monorepo is kebab-case, as it provides readability, consistency with tools, and URL-friendliness. For code consistency, camelCase can be used for variables and functions.
reason: The affirmative side provides a clear and consistent naming strategy that aligns with the decision drivers of providing consistency and making an early decision. Kebab-case is widely recognized for its readability and URL-friendliness, which are important factors for file and directory naming in a monorepo. The negative side's suggestion of using PascalCase for class/component files introduces complexity by requiring a dual approach, which may not be necessary given the primary focus on file and directory naming. The affirmative side's approach of using kebab-case for files and directories, with camelCase for variables and functions, offers a straightforward and consistent solution.
message_history:
Let's evaluate each naming convention based on the decision drivers and the context provided:

### Kebab-case

**Pros:**
- **Readability:** Kebab-case is often considered very readable, especially for file and directory names, as it uses hyphens to separate words, making it clear where one word ends and another begins.
- **URL-friendly:** It is commonly used in URLs, which can be beneficial if any file paths are exposed in web contexts.
- **Consistency with some tools:** Some tools and frameworks, especially in the JavaScript ecosystem, use kebab-case by default for file names.

**Cons:**
- **Not suitable for variables/functions:** It is not typically used for variable or function names in most programming languages, which can lead to inconsistency if used for both files and code.
- **Case sensitivity issues:** On some operating systems, case sensitivity can be an issue, though this is more of a general file system concern.

### PascalCase

**Pros:**
- **Common in class names:** PascalCase is widely used for class names in many programming languages, which can make it a natural choice for files that represent classes or components.
- **Clear word boundaries:** The capitalization of each word makes it easy to read and understand.

**Cons:**
- **Less common for file names:** It is less commonly used for file and directory names, which might make it less intuitive for some developers.
- **Inconsistent with URLs:** It is not URL-friendly, as URLs are case-insensitive and typically use lowercase.

### CamelCase

**Pros:**
- **Common for variables/functions:** CamelCase is widely used for variable and function names, which can make it consistent with the code inside the files.
- **Clear word boundaries:** Like PascalCase, it provides clear word boundaries through capitalization.

**Cons:**
- **Less common for file names:** Similar to PascalCase, it is not typically used for file and directory names.
- **Inconsistent with URLs:** It is not URL-friendly for the same reasons as PascalCase.

### Snake_case

**Pros:**
- **Readability:** Snake_case is also very readable, using underscores to separate words, which can be clear and easy to follow.
- **Consistency with some languages:** It is commonly used in languages like Python for both variables and file names, which can provide consistency.

**Cons:**
- **Less common in JavaScript/TypeScript:** It is not as commonly used in the JavaScript/TypeScript ecosystem, which might lead to inconsistency with other parts of the codebase.
- **Not URL-friendly:** Like PascalCase and CamelCase, it is not typically used in URLs.

### Selecting the Best Option

Given the context and decision drivers, the best option would likely be **kebab-case** for file and directory names. This choice provides a high level of readability and is consistent with many tools and frameworks in the JavaScript ecosystem. It is also URL-friendly, which can be beneficial if file paths are exposed in web contexts. While it may not be suitable for variable and function names, the focus here is on file and directory naming, where kebab-case excels. 

For code consistency, camelCase can be used for variables and functions, aligning with common JavaScript/TypeScript practices. This combination allows for a clear and consistent naming strategy across the monorepo.
While I understand the reasoning behind choosing kebab-case for file and directory names, I would like to present an alternative perspective that considers the broader context of the monorepo and the decision drivers.

### PascalCase

**Pros:**
- **Consistency with Class and Component Naming:** PascalCase is widely used for class and component names in many programming languages, including JavaScript/TypeScript. This can create a consistent naming convention across files that represent classes or components, making it easier for developers to identify the purpose of a file at a glance.
- **Clear Word Boundaries:** The capitalization of each word makes it easy to read and understand, which can be beneficial for both file names and directory names.

**Cons:**
- **Less Common for File Names:** While it is less commonly used for file and directory names, adopting PascalCase can create a clear distinction between files that represent classes/components and other types of files.
- **Inconsistent with URLs:** Although not URL-friendly, this is less of a concern if file paths are not directly exposed in web contexts.

### Selecting the Best Option

Given the decision drivers of providing consistency and making an early decision, I propose considering **PascalCase** for files and directories that represent classes or components. This aligns with common practices in JavaScript/TypeScript for naming classes and components, providing a consistent and intuitive naming strategy for developers familiar with these conventions.

For other types of files and directories, **kebab-case** can be used to maintain readability and URL-friendliness where necessary. This dual approach allows for a clear distinction between different types of files while maintaining consistency within each category.

In summary, while kebab-case is a strong contender for its readability and URL-friendliness, adopting PascalCase for class/component files can enhance consistency with code conventions, making it a viable option for the monorepo's naming strategy.

The negative side's suggestion of using PascalCase for class/component files introduces complexity by requiring a dual approach, which may not be necessary given the primary focus on file and directory naming. The affirmative side's approach of using kebab-case for files and directories, with camelCase for variables and functions, offers a straightforward and consistent solution.", "debate_answer": "The best option for file and directory naming in the monorepo is kebab-case, as it provides readability, consistency with tools, and URL-friendliness. For code consistency, camelCase can be used for variables and functions."}

## Context and problem statement

Artefactual have been working on a client application to provide easier access
to the functions exposed by the Archivematica Application Programming Interface
(API). This application is called [AMClient][amclient-1]. The application also
acts as a Python library which makes it easier to make Archivematica specific
API calls in code. The library was released as a [Python package][amclient-2]
early in 2019.

## Decision drivers

* Desire to simplify development processes in Archivematica and the Storage
  Service.
* Desire to make it easier to maintain components of Archivematica downstream
  and push the effects upstream (modularity).

## Considered options

1. Continue to make calls to the Archivematica API via low-level Python HTTP
   request libraries.
2. Replace low-level calls with a single consistent approach across the
   Archivematica code-base, i.e. use AMClient.
human_decision:
# Use AMClient (Archivematica API Client) for API calls in Archivematica

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: Artefactual Systems Inc.
* Date: 2019-10-25

## Decision outcome

Option 2. The AMClient library helps us to create consistency across the
Archivematica code-base. It is already widely used in the default Automation
Tools, and Archivematica Automated Acceptance Tests (AMAUAT).

### Positive consequences

* As the AMClient library is adapted as a Python package its use on the command
  line is extended and so it becomes more useful to those embedding it outside
  of Archivematica.
* As AMClient is extended, tests are added to the package itself, providing a
  greater level of lower-level testing, thusly the Archivematica API. The focus
  of testing in Archivematica can be on manipulating well understood AMClient
  responses where this is appropriate for the unit being tested.
* The number of lines of code required to talk to the AMClient package versus
  that of creating lower-level API calls via a HTTP requests library should be
  fewer.
* Missing functionality, e.g. as described by issue [#905][amclient-3] can be
  added as it is required by Archivematica and the Storage Service. As an
  external package, and something used inside Archivematica, the two take on a
  symbiotic relationship.

### Negative consequences

* The release candidate for version 1.0.0 of AMClient does not yet adhere to
  more traditional Python best practices i.e. it might not be considered to be
  entirely "Pythonic" in how it exposes errors to the calling code. This is
  described in issue [#488][amclient-4]. This effect of not being 'Pythonic'
  may travel upstream to Archivematica and is something for maintainers to be
  aware of. Work on an AMClient 2.0.0 should replace these patterns and then
  cascading those changes down to Archivematica should still be easier than
  working with and replacing the calls that exist in Archivematica using a HTTP
  requests library.

## Links

* Epic-level issue describing this ADR and other pieces required to implement
  it: [#543][amclient-5].
* ADR 0001: [Unification of METS creation][amclient-6] sets a precedent for
  this work to take place.

[amclient-1]: https://github.com/artefactual-labs/amclient
[amclient-2]: https://pypi.org/project/amclient/
[amclient-3]: https://github.com/archivematica/Issues/issues/905
[amclient-4]: https://github.com/archivematica/Issues/issues/488
[amclient-5]: https://github.com/archivematica/Issues/issues/543
[amclient-6]: https://adr.archivematica.org/0001-unification-of-mets-creation.html
debate_answer: Option 2 (using AMClient) is the better choice as it aligns with the goals of reducing complexity and ensuring consistency across the codebase.
reason: The affirmative side provides a compelling argument that aligns with the decision drivers of simplifying development processes and enhancing modularity. The use of AMClient offers consistency, simplification, and modularity, which are crucial for maintaining a scalable and maintainable codebase. While the negative side highlights the flexibility and control of low-level libraries, the long-term benefits of a unified approach with AMClient outweigh the initial challenges of learning and dependency management.
message_history:
Let's evaluate the pros and cons of each option:

### Option 1: Continue to make calls to the Archivematica API via low-level Python HTTP request libraries.

**Pros:**
1. **Flexibility:** Developers have full control over the HTTP requests, allowing for customization and optimization specific to certain use cases.
2. **Familiarity:** Developers who are already accustomed to using low-level HTTP libraries may find it easier to continue using them without needing to learn a new library.
3. **No Additional Dependencies:** Avoids adding another dependency to the project, which can simplify dependency management and reduce potential conflicts.

**Cons:**
1. **Complexity:** Writing and maintaining low-level HTTP requests can be more complex and error-prone, especially as the codebase grows.
2. **Inconsistency:** Different developers might implement API calls in different ways, leading to inconsistencies across the codebase.
3. **Maintenance Burden:** More code to maintain, as each API call needs to be manually handled, which can increase the risk of bugs and technical debt.

### Option 2: Replace low-level calls with a single consistent approach across the Archivematica code-base, i.e., use AMClient.

**Pros:**
1. **Consistency:** Using a single library for API calls ensures a consistent approach across the codebase, which can improve readability and maintainability.
2. **Simplification:** AMClient abstracts the complexity of making API calls, which can simplify development and reduce the likelihood of errors.
3. **Modularity:** Encourages modularity by providing a dedicated library for API interactions, which aligns with the decision drivers.
4. **Community and Support:** As a dedicated library, AMClient may have community support, documentation, and updates that can be beneficial.

**Cons:**
1. **Learning Curve:** Developers need to learn how to use AMClient, which may require some initial investment in time and training.
2. **Dependency Management:** Introducing a new dependency can lead to potential version conflicts or require updates as the library evolves.
3. **Limited Flexibility:** AMClient may not support all use cases or allow for the same level of customization as low-level HTTP libraries.

### Conclusion

Considering the decision drivers, which emphasize simplifying development processes and enhancing modularity, Option 2 (using AMClient) appears to be the better choice. It aligns with the goals of reducing complexity and ensuring consistency across the codebase. While there is a learning curve and dependency management to consider, the long-term benefits of a simplified and modular approach outweigh the initial challenges.
While I understand the reasoning behind choosing Option 2, I would like to present a different perspective that might support sticking with Option 1.

### Option 1: Continue to make calls to the Archivematica API via low-level Python HTTP request libraries.

**Pros:**
1. **Flexibility and Control:** Developers can tailor HTTP requests to meet specific needs, which can be crucial for optimizing performance or handling unique cases.
2. **Avoids Dependency Overhead:** By not introducing a new library, the project remains free from potential dependency conflicts and the need for regular updates to accommodate changes in AMClient.
3. **Immediate Familiarity:** For teams already proficient with low-level HTTP libraries, this approach avoids the learning curve associated with adopting a new tool.

**Cons:**
1. **Complexity and Error-Prone:** The need to manually handle each API call can lead to increased complexity and a higher chance of errors, especially as the project scales.
2. **Inconsistency Risks:** Without a standardized approach, different developers might implement API calls differently, leading to inconsistencies.
3. **Increased Maintenance:** More code to manage can lead to a higher maintenance burden and potential technical debt.

### Option 2: Replace low-level calls with a single consistent approach across the Archivematica code-base, i.e., use AMClient.

**Pros:**
1. **Standardization and Consistency:** A unified approach to API calls can enhance code readability and maintainability.
2. **Simplified Development:** By abstracting the complexity of API interactions, AMClient can reduce the likelihood of errors and streamline development.
3. **Modularity and Alignment with Goals:** Using a dedicated library supports modularity and aligns with the decision drivers for simplifying processes.

**Cons:**
1. **Learning Curve and Training:** Developers need to invest time in learning AMClient, which could slow down initial development.
2. **Dependency Management Challenges:** Introducing a new dependency can lead to version conflicts and require ongoing updates.
3. **Potential Limitations:** AMClient might not offer the same level of customization or support for all use cases as low-level libraries.

### Conclusion

While Option 2 offers clear benefits in terms of consistency and simplification, Option 1 should not be dismissed outright. If the development team is already highly skilled with low-level HTTP libraries and the project requires a high degree of customization, sticking with Option 1 could be advantageous. It avoids the overhead of learning a new tool and managing additional dependencies, which can be significant considerations in certain contexts.

Ultimately, the best choice depends on the specific needs and capabilities of the development team, as well as the project's long-term goals. If flexibility and control are paramount, and the team is equipped to handle the complexity, Option 1 might still be a viable path. However, if the primary focus is on reducing complexity and ensuring consistency, Option 2 remains a strong contender.

## Context and Problem Statement

Creating a central factory has served us well, but has drawbacks, as
outlined in [ADR 013](013_Main_Factory.md). This document is about
exploring ways to mitigate those drawbacks, while keeping the benefits of
type-safety and an architecture based on the SOLID principles.

## Decision Drivers

* FunFunFactory is a long class with too many extension
	points for tests and exposing too many instances.
* Ideally, the class should not have any branching logic. Without
	branching logic, the only test we need for this class is that it
	produces the right instances. We do this with our edge-to-edge
	(integration) tests.
* We will switch our web framework layer from the discontinued Silex
	framework to Symfony. Symfony comes with a powerful [Dependency
	Injection Container
	(DIC)](https://symfony.com/doc/current/components/dependency_injection.html)
	that has the same purpose as our factory.


## Considered Options

* Refactor FunFunFactory, inject it as a service into the controllers.
* Drop FunFunFactory and create Symfony DI service configurations instead
human_decision:
# Object Construction Improvements

Date: 2020-04-30

Deciders: Gabriel Birke, Abban Dunne, Corinna Hillebrand

## Status

Proposed

## Decision Outcome

The presented options are don't exclude each other, they are  the start
and end state of a refactoring. In reality, we will start with injecting
the FunFunFactory (without refactoring it) into the controllers. The next
step is to swap the factory and inject the services instead, defining the
FunFunFactory as the service creating factory in the service configuration
file. Over time, we will define more and more services in the service
configuration, extracting them from FunFunFactory, until we can delete it. 

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Refactor FunFunFactory

At the moment, the FunFunFactory does too much:

* Provide presentation services for the controllers
* Provide use cases for the controllers, across bounded contexts
* Provide "shared" services (logging, notification, entity manager) for
	all bounded contexts.

We could split the big factory into smaller ones - a `PresenterFactory`
for the controllers, a `SharedServicesFactory` for the shared services,
and a `ContextFactory` for each bounded context, with the
`SharedServicesFactory` passed in as a dependency.

We could either forgo the default Symfony application structure, trying to
build the controllers with a `ControllerFactory` integrated in a custom
front controller.

Alternatively, we could inject the factories as dependencies into the
controllers.

* Good, because the slightly anemic `ContextFactory` classes that are
	already implemented will show the dependencies of each context better.
* Good, because we will have more explicit knowledge of the layers and
	contexts, with each one having a smaller public interface.
* Good, because we would still have type-safety and autocompletion
	everywhere.
* Bad, because each context will have its own interface for the
	`SharedServicesFactory`.
* Bad, because the setup hides the dependencies of the controllers,
	creating the ["Service Locator" anti-pattern](https://blog.ploeh.dk/2010/02/03/ServiceLocatorisanAnti-Pattern/).
* Bad, because it goes "against the grain" of the Symfony framework,
	creating mental load if we try to figure out how to use Symfony
	without its DIC component.

### Drop FunFunFactory, use Symfony DIC service configuration

* Good, because we can [visualize our dependency tree](https://symfony.com/doc/current/service_container/debug.html) instead of jumping from initialization to initialization function.
* Good, because we will have less boilerplate code. Most classes can use
	[autowiring](https://blog.ploeh.dk/2010/02/03/ServiceLocatorisanAnti-Pattern/), we only need to define which interfaces use which concrete implementation.
* Good, because the service configuration format offers a domain-specific language for advanced 
   dependency injection concepts like decorating services, having shared
   services, etc, making them explicit. In the FunFunFactory these
   patterns are implicit.
* Bad, because the PHP type system will no longer make sure that we inject
	the correct services. We can mitigate this by [linting the container
	configuration](https://symfony.com/doc/current/service_container.html#linting-service-definitions) during CI.
* Bad, because this is a "big bang" rewrite of a crucial part of the
	application. We don't have a good safety net if we miss something.
	The rewrite a highly iterative process with pitfalls.
* Bad, because we lose "debuggability" - we can no longer step through the
	initialization process of classes as easy as with the factory. To
	mitigate this, all building code with branches should be moved into
	dedicated, unit-tested factories.
debate_answer: Option 2: Drop FunFunFactory and create Symfony DI service configurations instead is the best solution.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for leveraging Symfony's Dependency Injection Container, which aligns with the strategic move to Symfony and offers long-term benefits such as simplification, integration, and community support. While the negative side highlights the advantages of a controlled transition and familiarity, the potential for reduced complexity and maintenance with Symfony's DI container outweighs the immediate challenges of migration and learning curve. The strategic alignment with Symfony and the robust features of its DI container make Option 2 the more forward-looking choice.
message_history:
Let's evaluate each option by listing their pros and cons, and then determine which solution might be the best fit given the context and problem statement.

### Option 1: Refactor FunFunFactory, inject it as a service into the controllers.

**Pros:**
1. **Familiarity:** Retains the existing factory pattern, which the team is already familiar with.
2. **Incremental Change:** Allows for a gradual refactor, minimizing disruption to the current codebase.
3. **Type-Safety:** Maintains type-safety by keeping the factory pattern intact.
4. **SOLID Principles:** Can be refactored to better adhere to SOLID principles, especially the Single Responsibility Principle by breaking down the long class into smaller, more manageable components.

**Cons:**
1. **Complexity:** The class is already long and complex, and refactoring might not fully address the issue of too many extension points and exposed instances.
2. **Maintenance:** Continues to require maintenance of a custom factory solution, which might not be as robust or feature-rich as Symfony's DI container.
3. **Branching Logic:** While the goal is to remove branching logic, achieving this might be challenging and could require significant refactoring effort.

### Option 2: Drop FunFunFactory and create Symfony DI service configurations instead.

**Pros:**
1. **Leverage Symfony Features:** Takes full advantage of Symfony's powerful Dependency Injection Container, which is well-documented and widely used.
2. **Simplification:** Reduces the need for a custom factory, simplifying the architecture and potentially reducing the codebase size.
3. **Integration:** Seamlessly integrates with Symfony, aligning with the decision to switch from Silex to Symfony.
4. **Community Support:** Benefits from community support and best practices associated with Symfony's DI container.

**Cons:**
1. **Learning Curve:** Requires the team to learn and adapt to Symfony's DI configuration, which might involve a learning curve.
2. **Migration Effort:** Involves a complete migration from the existing factory pattern, which could be time-consuming and require significant changes to the codebase.
3. **Loss of Customization:** May lose some custom behaviors or optimizations present in the current factory setup.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers and the context, the best solution appears to be **Option 2: Drop FunFunFactory and create Symfony DI service configurations instead**. This option aligns with the strategic move to Symfony, leverages the framework's built-in capabilities, and simplifies the architecture by removing the need for a custom factory. While there is a learning curve and migration effort involved, the long-term benefits of using a robust, community-supported DI container outweigh the drawbacks. This approach also addresses the issues of complexity and maintenance associated with the current FunFunFactory.
While I understand the reasoning behind choosing Option 2, I would like to present an argument in favor of Option 1: Refactor FunFunFactory, inject it as a service into the controllers.

### Reasons for Choosing Option 1

1. **Controlled Transition:** By refactoring the existing FunFunFactory, the team can manage the transition more smoothly. This approach allows for incremental changes, which can be less risky than a complete overhaul. It provides the opportunity to gradually improve the codebase while maintaining stability.

2. **Preservation of Custom Logic:** The current factory might have custom logic and optimizations that are specifically tailored to the application's needs. Refactoring allows the team to preserve and enhance these customizations rather than potentially losing them in a migration to Symfony's DI container.

3. **Reduced Immediate Disruption:** Refactoring the existing factory minimizes immediate disruption to the codebase. This is particularly important if there are ongoing projects or deadlines that could be impacted by a significant architectural change.

4. **Familiarity and Expertise:** The team is already familiar with the existing factory pattern. Leveraging this familiarity can reduce the learning curve and allow the team to focus on improving the factory's design rather than learning a new system.

5. **Gradual Adoption of Symfony Features:** While Symfony's DI container is powerful, the team can still gradually adopt its features over time. By refactoring the factory, the team can start integrating Symfony's DI features in a controlled manner, allowing for a smoother transition.

### Conclusion

While Option 2 offers the benefits of leveraging Symfony's DI container, Option 1 provides a more controlled and less disruptive path forward. It allows the team to retain valuable custom logic, reduce immediate risks, and gradually improve the codebase. Given these considerations, Option 1 might be the more pragmatic choice, especially if the team is looking to balance improvement with stability.

## Context and Problem Statement

Since there will be multiple services deployed in the Operate First environment
(ex. Jupyterhub, Argo, Superset, Observatorium, etc) distributed over various
namespaces, we need to be able to monitor them.

To do so we will use [Prometheus](https://prometheus.io/).
This will be deployed using the [ODH Operator](https://opendatahub.io/) (ODH
operator includes the Prometheus Operator to do this).

Normally, Prometheus Operator would have been able to monitor services across
namespaces using cluster-wide access, but the recent versions of Openshift have
an instance of Prometheus Operator pre-installed for cluster monitoring. As such we
cannot do a cluster-wide Prometheus Operator setup since this would conflict
with the cluster monitoring instance.

We also cannot use Operator Groups to do a multiple namespace installation,
since selection of multiple target namespaces using operator groups might be
[deprecated in a future release](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.6/operators/understanding/olm/olm-understanding-operatorgroups.html#olm-operatorgroups-target-namespace_olm-understanding-operatorgroups).

One possible solution is User Workload Monitoring, but it is still a
[feature preview in Openshift v4.5](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.5/monitoring/monitoring-your-own-services.html#enabling-monitoring-of-your-own-services_monitoring-your-own-services)
and no documentation is available for UWM in Openshift v4.6 yet.

So the main question here is how do we structure the
Prometheus-Operator/Prometheus Deployments in Operate First?

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Access to cluster-wide permissions
  * The Prometheus instance might not have cluster wide resource access
* Prometheus resource locations
  * Where do we keep the monitoring resources like servicemonitors/podmonitors?
  * Whose responsibility is it to create these monitoring resources?
* Complexity of Prometheus deployments
  * How many instances of Prometheus do we deploy?
  * Which namespaces should these Prometheus instances be deployed in?

## Considered Options

* Option 1:
  * Single Prometheus instance in a dedicated monitoring namespace
  * Servicemonitors/podmonitors can be kept in the monitoring namespace
  * Give Prometheus serviceaccount the roles to access services in other
    namespaces
* Option 2:
  * Each namespace with a service has an instance of Prometheus
  * All servicemonitors/podmonitors will need to be kept in their respective
    service namespaces
  * One main Prometheus will federate metrics from all the instances of Prometheus
* Option 3:
  * Single Prometheus instance in a dedicated monitoring namespace
  * Give Prometheus serviceaccount the clusterrole to access services and other
    monitoring resources cluster wide
  * Servicemonitors/podmonitors can be kept in any namespace
human_decision:
# Application Monitoring in Operate First using Prometheus

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: 4n4nd, anishasthana, hemajv, HumairAK, tumido
* Date: 2021-01-14

Technical Story: [issue-1](https://github.com/operate-first/blueprint/issues/8)
                 [issue-2](https://github.com/operate-first/apps/issues/57)

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: Option 1, because:
  * Set up is less complex than Option 2, only a single Prometheus instance is
    needed
  * No clusterroles are required like Option 3, only roles to access specific
    namespaces are required
debate_answer: Option 2 is the best solution given the context and problem statement, as it provides scalability, resilience, and enhanced security through namespace isolation.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for Option 2, emphasizing its scalability, resilience, and security benefits. The decentralized approach of having separate Prometheus instances per namespace reduces the risk of a single point of failure and enhances security by isolating namespaces. This approach also allows for better scalability as the number of services grows, which is crucial in a dynamic environment like Operate First. While Option 3 offers centralized management, the potential security risks and single point of failure concerns make Option 2 a more robust and future-proof choice.
message_history:
Let's evaluate each option by listing their pros and cons, and then determine which might be the best solution given the context and problem statement.

### Option 1:
- **Single Prometheus instance in a dedicated monitoring namespace**
- **Servicemonitors/podmonitors can be kept in the monitoring namespace**
- **Give Prometheus serviceaccount the roles to access services in other namespaces**

**Pros:**
1. **Simplicity:** Having a single Prometheus instance reduces the complexity of managing multiple instances.
2. **Centralized Management:** All monitoring configurations and resources are centralized, making it easier to manage and update.
3. **Resource Efficiency:** A single instance may use fewer resources compared to multiple instances.

**Cons:**
1. **Limited Access:** The Prometheus instance might not have full cluster-wide access, potentially limiting its ability to monitor all necessary resources.
2. **Scalability Concerns:** A single instance might become a bottleneck if the number of services and namespaces grows significantly.
3. **Potential Security Risks:** Granting roles to access multiple namespaces could pose security risks if not managed properly.

### Option 2:
- **Each namespace with a service has an instance of Prometheus**
- **All servicemonitors/podmonitors will need to be kept in their respective service namespaces**
- **One main Prometheus will federate metrics from all the instances of Prometheus**

**Pros:**
1. **Namespace Isolation:** Each namespace has its own Prometheus instance, which can enhance security and isolation.
2. **Scalability:** This approach can scale well as each namespace manages its own monitoring.
3. **Resilience:** Failure in one Prometheus instance does not affect the monitoring of other namespaces.

**Cons:**
1. **Complexity:** Managing multiple Prometheus instances can be complex and require more administrative overhead.
2. **Resource Intensive:** Running multiple instances can consume more resources.
3. **Configuration Overhead:** Each namespace requires its own configuration, which can lead to duplication of effort.

### Option 3:
- **Single Prometheus instance in a dedicated monitoring namespace**
- **Give Prometheus serviceaccount the clusterrole to access services and other monitoring resources cluster wide**
- **Servicemonitors/podmonitors can be kept in any namespace**

**Pros:**
1. **Comprehensive Access:** Clusterrole access allows monitoring of all services and resources across the cluster.
2. **Centralized Management:** Similar to Option 1, it allows for centralized management of monitoring resources.
3. **Flexibility:** Servicemonitors/podmonitors can be placed in any namespace, providing flexibility in resource management.

**Cons:**
1. **Security Concerns:** Granting cluster-wide access can pose significant security risks if not properly controlled.
2. **Single Point of Failure:** Like Option 1, a single instance could become a bottleneck or point of failure.
3. **Potential Complexity:** Managing cluster-wide access and ensuring security can add complexity.

### Conclusion:

Given the context and problem statement, **Option 3** seems to be the most balanced solution. It provides comprehensive access to monitor all services across the cluster while maintaining centralized management. However, it is crucial to implement strict security measures to mitigate the risks associated with cluster-wide access. This option also offers flexibility in managing monitoring resources, which can be beneficial as the environment evolves.
While Option 3 does offer comprehensive access and centralized management, I would argue that **Option 2** might be a more suitable choice given the context and problem statement. Here's why:

### Option 2:
- **Each namespace with a service has an instance of Prometheus**
- **All servicemonitors/podmonitors will need to be kept in their respective service namespaces**
- **One main Prometheus will federate metrics from all the instances of Prometheus**

**Pros:**
1. **Namespace Isolation:** Each namespace having its own Prometheus instance enhances security and isolation, reducing the risk of a single point of failure affecting the entire monitoring system.
2. **Scalability:** This approach scales well as each namespace manages its own monitoring, allowing for growth without overloading a single Prometheus instance.
3. **Resilience:** If one Prometheus instance fails, it does not impact the monitoring of other namespaces, ensuring continued monitoring availability.

**Cons:**
1. **Complexity:** Managing multiple Prometheus instances can be complex and require more administrative overhead, but this can be mitigated with automation and proper tooling.
2. **Resource Intensive:** Running multiple instances can consume more resources, but this trade-off can be justified by the increased resilience and scalability.
3. **Configuration Overhead:** Each namespace requires its own configuration, which can lead to duplication of effort, but this can be streamlined with templates and shared configurations.

### Reasons for Choosing Option 2:

1. **Security and Isolation:** In environments where security and isolation are critical, having separate Prometheus instances per namespace reduces the risk of cross-namespace access and potential security breaches.

2. **Scalability and Resilience:** As the number of services and namespaces grows, Option 2 provides a scalable solution that can handle increased load without becoming a bottleneck. The resilience offered by this approach ensures that monitoring continues even if individual instances encounter issues.

3. **Future-Proofing:** With the potential deprecation of certain features in future OpenShift releases, having a decentralized approach might offer more flexibility and adaptability to changes in the platform.

4. **Federation Flexibility:** The use of a main Prometheus instance to federate metrics allows for centralized reporting and analysis while maintaining the benefits of distributed monitoring.

While Option 3 offers centralized management and comprehensive access, the potential security risks and single point of failure concerns make Option 2 a more robust and future-proof choice for the Operate First environment.

This approach also allows for better scalability as the number of services grows, which is crucial in a dynamic environment like Operate First. While Option 3 offers centralized management, the potential security risks and single point of failure concerns make Option 2 a more robust and future-proof choice.", "debate_answer": "Option 2 is the best solution given the context and problem statement, as it provides scalability, resilience, and enhanced security through namespace isolation."}
